steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage reports that snowball-sampling recruits (people recruited by social contacts) had much higher retention than people recruited by strangers. The blank must complete "suggesting that ___" with an inference that directly explains this retention gap. The correct answer must connect social connection to the willingness to stay in the study.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — social networks can become large enough that two people can share a network but nevertheless regard each other as strangers: This is a theoretical point about network size and stranger status. It doesn't explain why snowball-sampling participants had higher retention—it actually muddles the social-connection mechanism. ✗
  • Choice B — snowball sampling is more likely to improve retention rates among rural participants than among nonrural participants: The study was conducted only in rural counties, so there is no basis to compare rural vs. nonrural outcomes. This comparison goes beyond what the data support. ✗
  • Choice C — people with relatively small social networks are inherently less likely to be recruited to participate in a study via snowball sampling than are people with relatively large social networks: This addresses who gets recruited, not why recruited participants stay. It doesn't explain the retention difference at all. ✗
  • Choice D — being recruited to participate in a study by someone with whom one is socially connected may impart a feeling of obligation to persist with participation in the study: This directly explains the retention gap: social connection creates a sense of obligation that keeps participants from dropping out, which is exactly why snowball-sampling recruits stayed at higher rates. ✓
D is correct.
